CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear intro, method, experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (eligibility traces with DRQN, optimizer effects, and feasibility of forward-view traces) are stated early. The methodology and hyperparameters are described concretely (network architecture, trajectory lengths, games used), and limitations and related work are explicitly discussed. Notation and presentation seem consistent enough to follow, but the writing is characterized as only "adequate," suggesting minor clarity or presentation issues (e.g., some figures/tables or phrasing may not be fully polished). Overall it is well-written with only minor presentation flaws.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a notable and practically useful combination: applying forward-view Watkins Q(λ) eligibility traces to deep recurrent Q-networks with sub-trajectory replay, and demonstrates this is computationally feasible and beneficial (stabilizing learning and enabling DRQN to escape DQN’s failures such as in Tennis). The work clearly differentiates its contributions (trace integration with DRQN and the replay protocol) and adds empirical findings on optimizer choice (Adam vs RMSprop) that materially affect learning speed. While the methods are largely composed of known components, their non-trivial integration and the demonstrated empirical benefits constitute meaningful novelty rather than a purely incremental repetition.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are supported by targeted experiments: DRQN+forward‑view Watkins Q(λ) with sub‑trajectory replay improves stability and learning speed on Pong and notably enables DRQN to escape the poor repetitive policy in Tennis where DQN fails, and the optimizer comparison (Adam vs RMSprop) is empirically evaluated. Mathematical content appears limited but consistent with standard Q(λ) and DRQN formulations. However, the experimental evidence is narrow (only two Atari games, no mention of statistical significance) and forward‑view/truncation choices and replay off‑policy effects introduce applicability caveats. Overall the results are technically credible but somewhat limited in scope, warranting a minor‑issues score.

main_claims: Eligibility traces improve and stabilize learning when combined with deep recurrent Q-networks (DRQN) on Atari games.
Recurrent networks (DRQN) can escape poor repetitive policies that DQN gets stuck in (notably achieving near-optimal performance on Tennis where DQN fails).
The choice of optimizer has a strong effect: Adam substantially accelerates learning compared to RMSprop in these experiments.
Combining forward-view Watkins Q(λ) eligibility traces with sub-trajectory replay and DRQN is computationally feasible and effective.
_summary.methodology: Train a Deep Recurrent Q-Network (CNN -> dense -> LSTM -> linear Q outputs) using forward-view Watkins Q(λ) eligibility traces computed over sampled sub-trajectories (trajectory length 32, 10 filler steps, 22 training steps), comparing RMSprop and Adam optimizers on Atari games (Pong, Tennis).
metrics: average game score (measured as average over games during 125000 frames)
epochs to reach specified score (learning speed)
limitations: Experiments were conducted only on fully observable games (not on partially observable environments).
Forward-view eligibility traces are computationally demanding in general (requires storing transitions and applying network to each state), though mitigated here by sub-trajectory replay and DRQN.
Use of a frozen target network can limit the rate at which newly learned values are propagated (may constrain learning speed).
Trace computation is truncated once weights fall below 0.01 (effectively limiting trace length); this is an explicit experimental choice that may affect results.
Experience replay size may become problematic with increased learning speed (old observations may become too off-policy for traces).
_summary.topic_area: Reinforcement Learning - deep Q-networks (DRQN) with eligibility traces
